content: that is...not really a fitting word...

Looking at current text right now in English with random lowercase and uppercase letters and in Russian with sudden Огромная Огромная Огромная Огромная Сила (Huge ×4 Strength), I think it would be good at least to consider disabling the AI translation in the game.

From an operational standpoint, the current process creates additional work for official staff, who need to review and repeatedly correct the AI-messed-up-text. This reduces efficiency and adds unnecessary work.

While offering many languages is a positive feature, poor-quality AI translations negatively affect the game’s impression and may deter potential players. 

A more effective approach would be to focus on well-organized volunteer team and to recruit additional people specifically for languages with small amount of contributors.

Additionally I would like to propose an idea for improving our volunteer recruitment process. The current system sometimes results in insufficiently competent translators or simply conflict-prone individuals joining the team, which ultimately affects the quality of the project. To minimize these risks, I suggest delegating the initial recruitment to language-specific moderators.

Here is how the updated process could look:

Announcement: Publish information about the open recruitment on the official Discord server of the game. Candidates can reach out directly to the relevant moderator.

Application submission: The candidate fills out a short form (e.g., via Google Form or plain text). Required fields: Discord ID, contact email, in-game UID, and a brief summary of experience in translations or work in teams.

Evaluation and testing: The moderator reviews the application. If the form looks good, they give the candidate a test assignment.

Final approval: After the moderator successfully checks the test assignment, the candidate is referred to Ace for final review and approval.

The test assignment should be specifically written for this purpose so that candidates cannot look up answers online. The assignment should be offered in two languages — English and Chinese — so that the candidate can choose their preferred working language. However, only the sheet in the chosen language should be sent. This ensures that someone applying as a Chinese translator does not peek at the English version. This way, the candidate's actual skills become much clearer.

Why this system would be more effective:

- Reduced management burden: Ace workload will be significantly simplified, as the routine task of initial application filtering will fall on the moderators.

- Strict quality assessment: A moderator for a specific language is able to evaluate stylistic nuances and subtleties of translation much better and faster than a "centralized manager" who does not know all the localization languages.

- Overcoming language barriers: Communication during the selection stage will occur in the candidate’s and moderator’s native (or well known) language. This avoids misunderstandings caused by language barriers.

- Initial personal evaluation: Since the newcomer will ultimately work under that moderator, the initial contact allows for an immediate assessment of the candidate’s communication skills, reliability, and ability to accept feedback.

- Protection against AI / machine translation: A unique, context-dependent test assignment will enable moderators to quickly identify those who abuse machine translation. Experienced translators can spot such translation much more easily.

I’ve read your idea. That’s a really innovative step.

One thing comes in my mind though, shouldn’t we giving the test sheet in both Chinese and English? Because in Morimens sometimes CN and EN is both needed to have a good translation.

Providing both would be good for someone, who will translate from English text. This might show how person can work with unknown language.

As I mentioned in previous message, providing only Chinese text is necessary only for people, who claims to know Chinese. That will show how good they know this language, because if they see both CN and EN, EN text might help translator. That won't guarantee, that person shows their actual knowledge and skills.

Anyway test text shouldn't be from the game, it needs to be written specifically for test. One part of the text should look like skill description and another should looks like story text.

Also if anyone need help with proper organisation of language teams, feel free to ping me. I can show you how we did it in Russian team, so you can have an idea how it might look and figure out a better approach for other language teams <:ElyHi:1130728605102641183>
